quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of tamoxifen?
explanation: Tamoxifen competes with estradiol for estrogen receptors. In breast tissue, the tamoxifen-receptor complex is inactive — blocking estrogen-driven cancer cell growth. In bone and uterus, the complex has partial agonist activity — protecting bone density but increasing endometrial cancer risk.
wowFactor: Tamoxifen's tissue selectivity comes from how the receptor changes shape when tamoxifen (vs. estrogen) binds it — a small chemical difference creates completely different downstream effects.
statement: Tamoxifen competes with estradiol for receptors; in breast tissue the complex is inactive (cancer-blocking); in uterus it is a partial agonist (cancer risk).
visualDescription: Estrogen receptor with tamoxifen bound in breast (inactive complex), same receptor in uterus (partial agonist activation), cancer growth halted in breast

quizQuestion: What are the major adverse effects of tamoxifen?
explanation: Tamoxifen's estrogen agonist activity in the uterus increases endometrial cancer risk — requiring annual gynecologic exams and monitoring for abnormal vaginal bleeding. VTE (DVT/PE) risk is increased. Hot flashes occur from estrogen antagonism. Ophthalmologic effects (retinopathy) occur with long-term use.
wowFactor: A breast cancer drug that increases endometrial cancer risk — the benefit of preventing breast cancer recurrence must be weighed against a new cancer risk in another reproductive organ.
statement: Tamoxifen increases endometrial cancer and VTE risk; monitor for abnormal vaginal bleeding with annual gynecologic exams.

explanation: Annual gynecologic examination is required to screen for endometrial cancer. Teach patients to report any abnormal vaginal bleeding immediately. Counsel on increased VTE risk — avoid prolonged immobility. Annual ophthalmologic exam for retinopathy with long-term use.
wowFactor: Abnormal vaginal bleeding in a postmenopausal woman on tamoxifen is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise — immediate reporting is critical.

quizQuestion: What drug class does combined oral contraceptive belong to?
explanation: Combined oral contraceptives (Ortho-Cyclen, Yaz) contain synthetic estrogen (ethinyl estradiol) and progestin. They suppress ovulation via negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, and have additional mechanisms (thickened cervical mucus, thinned endometrium).
wowFactor: The pill works by convincing the brain that the woman is already pregnant — high hormone levels suppress the FSH and LH surge needed for ovulation.
statement: Combined oral contraceptives contain estrogen and progestin that suppress ovulation via hypothalamic-pituitary feedback inhibition.
visualDescription: Pill pack (estrogen and progestin), hypothalamic-pituitary axis with suppressed FSH and LH, no ovulation diagram

quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of combined oral contraceptives?
explanation: Combined OCs deliver constant exogenous estrogen and progestin, which suppress GnRH (from the hypothalamus) and FSH/LH (from the pituitary) via negative feedback. Without the LH surge, ovulation cannot occur. Additional effects: thickened cervical mucus and thinner endometrium.
wowFactor: The pill has three redundant mechanisms — suppressing ovulation, blocking sperm with thick cervical mucus, AND preventing implantation with thin endometrium — making it >99% effective when taken correctly.
statement: Combined OCs suppress FSH/LH via negative feedback, preventing the LH surge needed for ovulation.
visualDescription: Hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis with exogenous estrogen/progestin suppressing FSH/LH, no LH surge, ovulation absent

quizQuestion: What are the major adverse effects of combined oral contraceptives?
explanation: Estrogen increases clotting factor production, raising VTE risk (DVT, PE). Other effects: hypertension (common), nausea, breast tenderness, headache, and breakthrough bleeding. CONTRAINDICATED in smokers >35 years old, history of VTE, migraine with aura, and breast cancer.
wowFactor: The VTE risk from combined OCs is real but context-dependent: it is lower than the VTE risk of pregnancy itself — the risk of NOT using contraception may actually be higher.
statement: Combined OCs increase VTE and hypertension risk; contraindicated in smokers >35, history of VTE, or migraine with aura.
visualDescription: DVT deep vein clot diagram, BP cuff showing elevated reading, contraindication list: smoker >35, VTE history, migraine with aura

quizQuestion: Which drug most significantly reduces the effectiveness of combined oral contraceptives?
explanation: Rifampin is a potent CYP3A4 inducer — it dramatically accelerates the metabolism of ethinyl estradiol and progestin, reducing their plasma concentrations and contraceptive effectiveness. Patients must use backup contraception. Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine) also reduce OC efficacy.
wowFactor: A tuberculosis drug (rifampin) can cause an unintended pregnancy — nurses must counsel any woman on rifampin to use a backup contraceptive method immediately.
statement: Rifampin induces CYP3A4, accelerating OC metabolism and reducing contraceptive effectiveness; backup method required.
visualDescription: Rifampin pill with CYP3A4 enzyme inducing rapid OC breakdown, contraceptive effectiveness dropping, backup contraception sign

quizQuestion: What nursing education points are most important for a patient starting combined oral contraceptives?
explanation: Take at the SAME TIME every day (consistency maintains hormone levels). Teach ACHES warning signs: Abdominal pain (DVT/PE), Chest pain (MI/PE), Headache (stroke), Eye changes (stroke), Severe leg pain (DVT). Contraindicated in smokers >35 (stroke risk). Many drug interactions reduce efficacy.
wowFactor: The mnemonic ACHES — Abdominal pain, Chest pain, Headache, Eye changes, Severe leg pain — helps patients identify the serious thrombotic complications that require immediate medical attention.
statement: Teach ACHES warning signs, take at same time daily, and screen for contraindications including smoking over age 35.

quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of docusate?
explanation: Docusate is an anionic surfactant. It lowers the surface tension of the fecal mass, allowing water and fat to mix into stool and soften it. This is a purely physical mechanism — it does not stimulate peristalsis.
wowFactor: Docusate works exactly like dish soap — reducing surface tension so water penetrates the stool, the same chemistry that lets soap cut through grease.
statement: Docusate acts as a surfactant, reducing fecal surface tension so water and fat penetrate and soften stool.

quizQuestion: What are the primary indications for ipratropium?
explanation: Ipratropium is first-line for COPD maintenance (works well in the cholinergic-dominant COPD airways) and is used adjunctively with albuterol in severe asthma exacerbations (combined in DuoNeb). It is not a rescue inhaler.
wowFactor: COPD airways have relatively more cholinergic tone than asthma airways — which is exactly why anticholinergics like ipratropium work better for COPD maintenance than for asthma.
statement: Ipratropium is the first-line inhaled bronchodilator for COPD maintenance and adjunct therapy in severe asthma.

quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of loperamide?
explanation: Loperamide activates mu-opioid receptors in the enteric nervous system, slowing intestinal peristalsis and reducing fluid secretion. Its P-glycoprotein efflux and poor BBB penetration prevent CNS opioid effects at therapeutic doses.
wowFactor: Loperamide is an opioid that P-glycoprotein actively pumps out of the brain — a cleverly engineered antidiarrheal that cannot cause CNS opioid effects.
statement: Loperamide activates enteric mu-opioid receptors to slow intestinal motility and reduce fluid secretion without CNS effects.

quizQuestion: What is the most serious adverse effect of high-dose pancrelipase?
explanation: GI upset (nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea) is common. The most serious adverse effect is fibrosing colonopathy — colonic strictures seen primarily in cystic fibrosis patients on very high lipase doses (>10,000 units/kg/day).
wowFactor: High-dose pancrelipase can scar the colon — the very drug that keeps cystic fibrosis patients nourished can harm their gut if dosed excessively.
statement: Pancrelipase causes GI upset; fibrosing colonopathy is a serious risk at very high doses in cystic fibrosis patients.

correctAnswer: Ulcerative colitis; Crohn disease; rheumatoid arthritis
quizQuestion: What are the primary indications for sulfasalazine?
explanation: Sulfasalazine treats inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease) via local 5-ASA anti-inflammatory activity in the colon. It is also used as a DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis via systemic sulfapyridine effects.
wowFactor: One drug, two completely different diseases — 5-ASA treats gut inflammation in IBD, while the sulfapyridine component travels systemically to suppress joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
statement: Sulfasalazine treats ulcerative colitis, Crohn disease, and rheumatoid arthritis via its two active components.
visualDescription: Split panel: inflamed colon with 5-ASA treating IBD, inflamed joint with sulfapyridine treating RA
